questions.questionText: A patient develops circumoral numbness, metallic taste, and then seizures after receiving a large dose of lidocaine local anesthesia. The most appropriate treatment is:
questions.optionB: 20% Intralipid (lipid emulsion) infusion — rescues cardiac and CNS toxicity of local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST)
questions.explanation: Core concept: LAST treatment = Intralipid 20% infusion (lipid rescue). Why correct: LA toxicity: CNS (circumoral numbness → metallic taste → tinnitus → visual disturbance → seizures → coma) → CVS (hypotension → bradycardia → arrhythmias → cardiac arrest). 20% Intralipid: acts as 'lipid sink' — extracts lipophilic LA from tissues/cardiac sodium channels. Also: stop LA injection, secure airway, benzodiazepines for seizures, CPR if arrest. Why A wrong: Adrenaline is used in CPR but Intralipid is the SPECIFIC antidote for LAST. Why C wrong: Naloxone = opioid antagonist. Why D wrong: Flumazenil = benzodiazepine antagonist. Clinical relevance: Bupivacaine is most cardiotoxic LA (binds strongly to cardiac Na+ channels). Ropivacaine is less cardiotoxic alternative. Exam trap: CNS toxicity occurs BEFORE cardiac toxicity (CNS more sensitive to LA). Memory aid: 'LAST = Lipid emulsion Antidote Saves from Toxicity.' Reference: Aitkenhead – LA Toxicity.
questions.conceptTag: LAST lipid rescue Intralipid bupivacaine toxicity

questions.questionText: Which local anesthetic has the fastest onset of action?
questions.optionB: Lidocaine (pKa closest to physiological pH 7.4 → highest proportion of un-ionized form at body pH → fastest membrane penetration → fastest onset)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Onset of LA depends on pKa — closer to body pH = faster onset. Why correct: Lidocaine pKa = 7.9 (closest to 7.4 among common agents) → ~25% un-ionized at physiological pH → faster nerve penetration. Bupivacaine pKa 8.1, ropivacaine 8.1 — slower onset. Why A wrong: Bupivacaine — slow onset but LONG duration (highest protein binding). Why C wrong: Ropivacaine — similar to bupivacaine, less cardiotoxic, slower onset. Why D wrong: Tetracaine — slow onset, used topically. Clinical relevance: Un-ionized form crosses membrane; ionized form blocks Na+ channel from inside. Acidic tissues (infection/abscess) → more ionized LA → reduced efficacy → 'LA doesn't work in infected tissue.' Exam trap: Onset = pKa, Duration = protein binding, Potency = lipid solubility. The pharmacology triad of LA. Memory aid: 'Onset = pKa (Low pKa = fast Onset); Duration = Protein binding; Potency = Lipid solubility.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Local Anesthetics.

questions.questionText: Adrenaline is added to local anesthetics for all of the following reasons EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: Increases rate of systemic absorption (adrenaline causes VASOCONSTRICTION, which DECREASES systemic absorption, not increases)
questions.optionD: Increases maximum safe dose of LA
questions.explanation: Core concept: Adrenaline (epinephrine) added to LA causes vasoconstriction → slower absorption → longer duration. Why correct: Adrenaline DECREASES (not increases) systemic absorption by causing local vasoconstriction. Benefits: prolongs block duration, reduces peak plasma levels (safer), decreases bleeding, allows higher total dose. Why A wrong: TRUE — vasoconstriction keeps LA at nerve longer. Why C wrong: TRUE — vasoconstricted vessels bleed less. Why D wrong: TRUE — slower absorption → lower peak levels → higher total dose safe. Clinical relevance: Contraindicated in ring blocks (fingers, toes, ears, nose, penis) — risk of ischemic necrosis in end-artery territories. Max dose lidocaine: 3mg/kg without adrenaline, 7mg/kg with. Exam trap: 'Ring block' sites = NO adrenaline — this is very commonly tested. Memory aid: 'Adrenaline = Anti-absorption (vasoconstriction) → prolonged, safer, drier field.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Local Anesthetics.
